1.synthesis: At the culmination of the plague contest, before the death of the firstborn falls, God institutes the Passover — the festival that will define Israel's identity forever. The month of Abib becomes the first month; on the tenth day each household selects a lamb without blemish, a male a year old (vv.1–5). On the fourteenth day the whole assembly slaughters it at twilight. The blood is applied to the doorposts and lintel of the house; the lamb is roasted with fire and eaten with unleavened bread and bitter herbs in haste — 'your loins girded, your sandals on your feet, your staff in your hand. And you shall eat it in haste. It is the Lord's Passover' (v.11). The destroyer will pass through Egypt but pass over the blood-marked houses: 'I will pass over you, and no plague will befall you to destroy you, when I strike the land of Egypt' (v.13). The feast of Unleavened Bread — seven days, beginning the fifteenth — is decreed for all generations (vv.15–20).
1.historical_context: Keil and Delitzsch observe that the Passover was 'a divine consecration necessary, that Israel's outward severance from Egypt might be accompanied by an inward severance from everything of an Egyptian or heathen nature.' The lamb regulations have precise sacrificial specifications: 'without blemish' (tamim), 'a male a year old' — the standard for valid Levitical sacrifice. The blood application with hyssop on doorposts and lintel creates a symbolic protective threshold. Gill notes the timing of the institution — before the darkness plague, organized here narratively after the plague account to keep the Passover legislation together. The month-change in verse 2 (Nisan becomes 'first month') reorders Israel's calendar around the Exodus event: redemption rather than creation becomes the annual starting point. The feast of Unleavened Bread with its seven-day removal of leaven enacts total separation from the old life of slavery.
1.literary_structure: The Passover pericope (vv.1–20) is the legislative center of Exodus, structured as direct divine speech to Moses and Aaron. It moves from the calendar change (vv.1–2) through the lamb regulations (vv.3–10) to the night's eating instructions (vv.11–13) to the permanent commemorative ordinance (vv.14–20). The phrase 'it is the Lord's Passover' (v.11) names the rite theologically rather than merely descriptively: this is not Israel's ritual but God's own provision. The connection between the blood and survival — 'when I see the blood, I will pass over you' (v.13) — makes the blood the sole criterion of protection; no other identity marker, merit, or lineage provides shelter from the destroyer.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry on the blood as the sole covering: 'The blood of the lamb was the distinction between the firstborn of Israel and the firstborn of Egypt; not that they were better than others, but that God had put a difference by his free grace.' Gill on the paschal lamb's specifications: the male 'without blemish' anticipates the legal requirements later codified in Leviticus, which the New Testament explicitly applies to Christ. The bitter herbs recall Egypt's bondage; the unleavened bread enacts haste and separation. Adam Clarke on the calendar reorientation: the change of the year's beginning in verse 2 signals that for Israel, time itself is henceforth reckoned from redemption — 'a new era, a new time, a new world, for those who are redeemed by the blood of the Lamb.' The feast of Unleavened Bread's seven days enforce what Paul calls 'cleaning out the old leaven' (1 Corinthians 5:7–8).
1.christology: Paul identifies the paschal lamb directly with Christ: 'For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed' (1 Corinthians 5:7). John the Baptist's 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world' (John 1:29) translates the Passover typology into incarnate reality. The blood that causes the destroyer to 'pass over' is the blood of the one who is without blemish (1 Peter 1:19: 'like that of a lamb without blemish or spot'). The eating with loins girded, sandals on feet, and staff in hand — the posture of those ready to depart — is the posture of pilgrims who know they are not yet home, whose citizenship is in heaven (Philippians 3:20).

21.synthesis: Moses gathers the elders of Israel and transmits the Passover instructions for immediate action: 'Select lambs for yourselves according to your clans, and kill the Passover lamb. Take a bunch of hyssop and dip it in the blood that is in the basin, and touch the lintel and the two doorposts with the blood that is in the basin. None of you shall go out of the door of his house until the morning' (vv.21–22). The Lord will pass through to strike Egypt; when he sees the blood, 'the Lord will pass over the door and will not allow the destroyer to enter your houses to strike you' (v.23). The rite is to be kept as an ordinance forever; when children ask 'What do you mean by this service?' parents are to explain: 'It is the sacrifice of the Lord's Passover' (vv.26–27). The people bow their heads and worship; they do as commanded (v.28).
21.historical_context: Keil and Delitzsch on the hyssop bundle: the plant (azob) was probably not European hyssop but a species of origanum or thyme common in Arabia and Sinai, used elsewhere in Levitical purification rites (Leviticus 14:51; Numbers 19:18). The basin (saf) catches the blood immediately after the animal's death. The command not to leave the house until morning (v.22) is the critical protective instruction: 'in this night of judgment there would be no safety anywhere except behind the blood-stained door' (Keil). The catechetical question-and-answer of verses 26–27 is one of the earliest instances of liturgical instruction built into a rite from its institution: the observance itself creates the occasion for the parents' explanation, which transfers memory across generations. The people's immediate bowing and obedience (v.28) stands in stark contrast to their prior murmuring and sets the Passover night apart as a moment of united covenant compliance.
21.literary_structure: The section (vv.21–28) moves from ritual instruction (vv.21–23) through the theological explanation of the destroyer's action (v.23) to the generational transmission command (vv.24–27) and the people's response (v.28). The structure mirrors the pedagogical purpose of the feast itself: the parents' action (vv.21–23) becomes the basis for the children's question (v.26) which generates the parents' proclamation (v.27). The destroyer (hamashchit) is distinguished from God himself: God passes through, but the destroyer is the agent of death — a distinction that preserves the holiness of the divine while acknowledging the reality of the judgment-agent deployed.
21.theological_application: Matthew Henry on the hyssop: 'The hyssop of faith, dipped in the blood of Christ, and applied to the soul and conscience, purges from dead works.' Gill on the door's protective function: 'Not because there was any virtue in the blood to keep off the destroying angel, any more than there was in the brazen serpent to heal the Israelites; but as both were typical of Christ, the virtue was in him, and in his blood.' The catechetical purpose (vv.26–27) shows that God designs his covenant ordinances to be pedagogically self-explanatory — the rite carries its own explanation built in, so that the community's worship is simultaneously its instruction. The people's bowing (v.28) is worship prior to deliverance: they believe the promise and act on it before the plague falls, which is the essence of the obedience of faith (Hebrews 11:28: 'by faith he kept the Passover and sprinkled the blood').
21.christology: 'None of you shall go out of the door of his house until morning' (v.22): the church sheltered under the blood of Christ does not venture into the realm of judgment — the night of this world — under its own initiative, but remains within the covering of the one through whose blood the destroyer has no claim. Keil's observation stands: 'there would be no safety anywhere except behind the blood-stained door.' The blood on the lintel and doorposts forms the shape that later typology sees as the cross — the four points of contact (top, left, right, threshold) creating the covering that marks the house as belonging to the God who spares. The destroyer cannot enter where the blood has been applied; death has no dominion over those sheltered in the crucified and risen Christ (Romans 6:9).

29.synthesis: 'At midnight the Lord struck down all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sat on his throne to the firstborn of the captive who was in the dungeon, and all the firstborn of the livestock' (v.29). Egypt erupts in a great cry; there is not a house without someone dead. Pharaoh rises in the night and summons Moses and Aaron: 'Up, go out from among my people, both you and the people of Israel; and go, serve the Lord, as you have said. Take your flocks and your herds, as you have said, and be gone, and bless me also!' (vv.31–32). The Egyptians urge the Israelites to leave at once: 'We shall all be dead.' The Israelites take their dough before it is leavened, and receive from the Egyptians silver, gold, and clothing — 'they plundered the Egyptians' (v.36).
29.historical_context: Keil and Delitzsch observe that the plague fell at midnight 'when all Egypt was lying in deep sleep... to startle the king and his people out of their sleep of sin.' The death extended from the greatest to the least — from the heir to the throne to the prisoner in the dungeon — as Moses had warned (Exodus 11:5), showing that no social status or innocence of person averted the judgment (the dungeon prisoner was not being punished for anything). The Egyptians' demand that Israel 'bless them' (v.32) is a startling reversal: the nation that cursed and enslaved Israel now begs a blessing from the departing people of God, reminiscent of the Philistines returning the ark (1 Samuel 6:3). The plundering of Egypt (v.36) fulfills the promise of Exodus 3:21–22 and Genesis 15:14: 'afterwards they shall come out with great possessions.'
29.literary_structure: The section pivots from the dead of Egypt to the departure of Israel with a rapid narrative pace that matches the night's urgency. Three scenes unfold: (1) the stroke at midnight and Egypt's cry (vv.29–30); (2) Pharaoh's summoning of Moses and Aaron and his capitulation — granting everything previously refused, including livestock and blessings (vv.31–32); (3) the Egyptians' urging departure and the material transfer (vv.33–36). The structural irony is complete: Pharaoh, who asked 'Who is the Lord, that I should obey his voice and let Israel go?' (Exodus 5:2), now begs Moses to take everything and 'bless me also.' The spoiling of Egypt is not a theft but a restitution.
29.theological_application: Keil and Delitzsch on the scope of the death: 'as all the previous plagues rested upon a natural basis, it might seem probable that this was also the case here — but the miracle consisted in the fact that the firstborn both of man and beast, and they alone, were all suddenly slain.' Matthew Henry on the dungeon prisoner's firstborn dying: 'The prince was not too high to be reached by it, nor were the slaves at the mill too low to be taken notice of... none are exempted.' Gill on Pharaoh's reversal: 'He who had hardened his heart so many times now urgently presses the very departure he had so often refused, and adds what he had never said before — "and bless me also." What a striking proof that the heart of the king is in God's hand.' The plundering of Egypt (v.36) is not vengeance but compensation — 430 years of unpaid labor receiving its due.
29.christology: The death of Egypt's firstborn at midnight is the event that gives the Passover — and hence the cross — its meaning. God's wrath passes over the blood-covered house; it falls on every unprotected firstborn in Egypt. The typological logic Paul articulates in 1 Corinthians 5:7 runs directly here: the only firstborn that died in Israel that night was the firstborn who was the lamb itself. The one who takes the penalty so that the household is spared — the substitutionary lamb — is the type of which Christ is the antitype. God's own firstborn-and-beloved (Exodus 4:22: 'Israel is my firstborn son') goes free because the Son of God takes the midnight death in his own person, so that those sheltered in him are permanently passed over.

37.synthesis: 'And the people of Israel journeyed from Rameses to Succoth, about six hundred thousand men on foot, besides women and children. A mixed multitude also went up with them, and very much livestock, both flocks and herds' (vv.37–38). The Israelites had baked unleavened bread from the dough they carried out of Egypt; 'they could not wait, nor had they prepared any provisions for themselves' (v.39). The sojourn in Egypt had lasted 430 years to the day (vv.40–41); 'it was a night of watching by the Lord, to bring them out of the land of Egypt; so this same night is a night of watching kept to the Lord by all the people of Israel throughout their generations' (v.42).
37.historical_context: The figure of 600,000 men on foot — exclusive of women, children, and the elderly — implies a total population of possibly two million or more. Matthew Henry notes: 'What a vast increase was this, to arise from seventy souls in little more than 200 years' time!' The mixed multitude (ereb rab) was the non-Israelite contingent who witnessed the plagues and joined the Exodus — probably including Egyptians who feared the Lord (Exodus 9:20), foreign slaves, and intermarried persons. Their presence is later a source of spiritual trouble in the wilderness (Numbers 11:4). The 430-year figure corresponds to the LXX's 'four hundred and thirty years' inclusive of patriarchal sojourn; Keil and Delitzsch harmonize this with the Genesis 15:13 figure ('four hundred years') as rounding to a generation marker. The 'night of watching' (leil shimmurim) makes the night of departure permanently sacred: each annual Passover night re-enacts this exodus-vigil.
37.literary_structure: The departure narrative is deliberately comprehensive: the number (v.37), the mixed company (v.38), the food situation (vv.39–41), and the theological framing (v.42) are all given. The 'to the day' precision of verse 41 signals that the 430 years was not approximate but exactly measured from the moment God first set it in motion — the covenant of Genesis 15:13–14 was being fulfilled with calendrical exactness. The 'night of watching' language at the close (v.42) transforms the historical event into a liturgical institution: Israel is not merely remembering what happened but participating annually in the same divine vigil that protected them.
37.theological_application: Matthew Henry on the night of watching: 'It was a night in which God kept a gracious eye upon his Israel, to guard and protect them; and a night in which they kept a wakeful eye upon his conduct and movements.' The fact that 430 years fulfilled 'to the day' demonstrates divine precision in providential timing: God counts the years of suffering; the Covenant keeper holds no detail in indifference. Gill on the mixed multitude: 'these had seen the plagues God brought on Egypt, and were convinced that the God of the Hebrews was the true God; and though they did not receive circumcision, they attached themselves to Israel — but their subsequent behavior shows the danger of a people of God being mixed with those who have not the same spiritual calling.' The unleavened bread eaten in haste (v.39) is the bread of affliction that becomes, in perpetual observance, the bread of liberation.
37.christology: 'A night of watching by the Lord' (v.42): the entire night God watched over his people, and they watched for him. Paul applies this structure to the Christian life in the Lord's Supper context: 'as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes' (1 Corinthians 11:26). The Eucharist is the church's night-of-watching — the sacred vigil between the first coming (the Passover death of Christ) and the second (the final Exodus into the presence of God). The mixed multitude who join Israel's departure foreshadow the nations who, through the new Passover, are incorporated into the redeemed people of God by faith (Galatians 3:28).

43.synthesis: God gives supplementary regulations for Passover participation: 'No foreigner shall eat of it, but every slave that is bought for money may eat of it after you have circumcised him. No temporary resident or hired worker shall eat of it' (vv.43–45). The lamb must be eaten inside a single house; no bone shall be broken; the whole congregation of Israel must keep it (vv.46–47). The foreigner who wishes to keep the Passover must circumcise all males in his household first; 'he shall be as a native of the land. But no uncircumcised person shall eat of it' (v.48). 'There shall be one law for the native and for the stranger who sojourns among you' (v.49). The people do as commanded; the Lord brings Israel out of Egypt 'by their hosts' (v.51).
43.historical_context: Keil and Delitzsch explain the necessity of these supplementary regulations: the presence of the mixed multitude (v.38) had created a new social reality — Israel was now in contact with non-Israelites who needed clear guidance on their Passover status. The key principle is covenantal inclusion through circumcision rather than ethnic descent: 'It was by virtue of a divine call, and not through natural descent, that Israel had become the people of Jehovah.' Purchased slaves circumcised into the household are fully incorporated; temporary residents and hired workers — the same labor relationship Israel had experienced in Egypt — are excluded unless they undergo covenant incorporation. The 'no bone shall be broken' regulation (v.46) is cited in John 19:36 as fulfilled at the crucifixion: the soldiers, finding Jesus already dead, did not break his legs.
43.literary_structure: The regulations move through three circles of participation: the household slave (fully incorporated by circumcision, v.44); the temporary resident and hired worker (excluded unless circumcised, vv.45, 48); the native-born Israelite (default participant). The structural principle is covenant membership, not ethnicity — circumcision is the defining mark. The unifying law of verse 49 ('one law for native and stranger') establishes the universality of the covenant standard: God's people is not a biological but a covenantal entity, defined by the rite of inclusion rather than the accident of birth. The closing summary (vv.50–51) mirrors the opening of the Passover legislation — Israel does as commanded, and God executes his promised deliverance 'by their hosts,' the military language affirming that this departure is not flight but the march of God's army.
43.theological_application: Matthew Henry on the foreigner's inclusion by circumcision: 'The door of admission into the church was open to all, provided they come in by the door, which is circumcision — the covenant seal — and not by any other way.' Gill on the 'one law' principle: 'This shows that God was no respecter of persons; the same rule, the same privilege, and the same obligation applied to the proselyte as to the native Israelite.' The prohibition on hiring out the Passover participation (the hired worker excluded) signals that covenant membership is not a commercial transaction but a life commitment — one enters the covenant wholly, not temporarily. The no-broken-bones regulation (v.46) was not merely hygienic or aesthetic but proleptic: it preserved the physical integrity of the lamb so that the typological connection to the Christ who would die with unbroken bones (John 19:36) would be unmistakably clear.
43.christology: 'Not one of his bones will be broken' (John 19:36): John explicitly identifies the Passover regulation of Exodus 12:46 as fulfilled in the crucifixion. The soldiers' decision not to break Jesus's legs — because he was already dead — was not coincidental but providentially aligned with the original legislation. The Passover lamb must be physically whole, its structural integrity preserved, so that the type perfectly matches the antitype. The one-law principle for native and stranger in verse 49 receives its full realization in Christ, where 'there is neither Jew nor Greek... for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28) — the circumcision-barrier that once required proselytes to be incorporated through a rite is now replaced by the circumcision of the heart through baptism into Christ's death (Colossians 2:11–12).
